skill.name: medical-device-mdr-auditor
skill.description: Audit medical device technical files for compliance with EU MDR 2017/745 regulations. Check for required Clinical Evaluation Reports, Post-Market Surveillance plans, and supporting documentation.
skill.summary: Automated compliance auditing for EU MDR 2017/745 medical device technical documentation.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: Static analysis returned a risk score of 100 due to 85 pattern detections, but all patterns were evaluated and found to be false positives on full code review. The skill contains no cryptographic operations, no network requests, no credential handling, and no obfuscation. Markdown backtick formatting was mistaken for shell execution, and security checklist documentation was mistaken for path traversal. The Python script performs legitimate file read/write operations for its audit purpose. The only real concern is that the script writes to a user-specified output path, which is inherent to its function.

low_findings.title: File write to user-specified output path
low_findings.description: The script writes audit reports to a path specified by the --output flag with no path restriction enforcement. While this is standard for a local audit tool, users should verify the output path to avoid unintended file overwrites.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded documentation URL
low_findings.description: SKILL.md contains a hardcoded URL pointing to the source GitHub repository. This is standard documentation practice for open-source projects and poses no exfiltration risk since the skill makes no network requests.

low_findings.title: Security checklist not programmatically enforced
low_findings.description: SKILL.md documents a security checklist including input path validation and output restriction, but these protections rely on user discipline rather than enforced code-level guardrails. The script accepts arbitrary --output paths without sandboxing.

dangerous_patterns.title: Static analyzer: markdown backticks mistaken for shell execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The static analyzer detected backtick characters in SKILL.md and references/audit-reference.md as Ruby/shell backtick execution. These are markdown code span formatting characters used to display command examples in documentation, not executable code. Reviewed and dismissed as false positive.

dangerous_patterns.title: Static analyzer: security checklist items mistaken for path traversal
dangerous_patterns.description: Lines 226 and 229 of SKILL.md contain security checklist entries documenting path traversal protections ('No unauthorized file system access (../)' and 'Input file paths validated (no ../ traversal)'). The static analyzer incorrectly flagged the checklist text as actual path traversal sequences. Reviewed and dismissed as false positive.

dangerous_patterns.title: Static analyzer: no weak cryptographic algorithms found in code
dangerous_patterns.description: The static analyzer reported 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' at multiple locations across SKILL.md, scripts/main.py, and the audit result JSON. Full code review confirmed the skill performs no cryptographic operations of any kind. No hashlib, no encryption, and no hashing functions exist in scripts/main.py. These detections are false positives likely triggered by hex strings or hash-like values in evaluation data. Reviewed and dismissed as false positive.

dangerous_patterns.title: Static analyzer: heuristic combination flagged no real threat
dangerous_patterns.description: The static analyzer detected dangerous combinations of code execution, network, and credential access across multiple files. Review confirmed the script has no network access, no credential handling, and no data exfiltration. The analyzer correlated independent false-positive signals (markdown URLs, markdown backticks, and config dict access) into a misleading combination alert. Reviewed and dismissed as false positive.

content.user_title: Audit Medical Device Technical Files for EU MDR Compliance
content.value_statement: Medical device manufacturers must verify their technical documentation against EU MDR 2017/745 before regulatory submission. This skill automates the document compliance check, identifying missing Clinical Evaluation Reports, PMS Plans, and other required files by device class.
actual_capabilities: Audit medical device technical documentation against EU MDR 2017/745 document requirements by device class
Check for required documents including Clinical Evaluation Reports, Post-Market Surveillance Plans, and PMCF Plans
Classify devices by risk class (I, IIa, IIb, III) and apply the appropriate regulatory checklist
Generate structured compliance reports with findings categorized as critical, major, minor, or informational
Perform batch audits across multiple devices using a JSON configuration file
Inspect file contents for key regulatory sections such as clinical data assessment and risk-benefit conclusions
limitations: Cannot verify the clinical accuracy or scientific validity of medical claims within documents
PDF and DOCX text extraction is simplified and may miss content in tables, images, or complex formatting
File completeness checks rely on size heuristics and keyword matching, not full semantic content validation
Requires all documents to be accessible on the local filesystem

use_cases.title: Pre-submission document verification
use_cases.description: Verify that a technical file contains all required documents for the target device class before submitting to a notified body for MDR certification.
use_cases.target_user: Regulatory affairs specialists preparing MDR submissions

use_cases.title: Internal pre-audit gap analysis
use_cases.description: Run compliance checks across a product line to identify documentation gaps and prioritize remediation before scheduled external audits.
use_cases.target_user: Quality management system owners and internal auditors

use_cases.title: Portfolio-wide compliance screening
use_cases.description: Batch audit technical files for multiple devices across different classifications to assess overall organizational readiness for MDR transition.
use_cases.target_user: Clinical evaluation team leads and regulatory program managers

prompt_templates.title: Basic single-device audit
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit the technical file at /path/to/device-folder for a Class IIa medical device against EU MDR 2017/745. Generate a compliance report showing which documents are present, missing, or incomplete.
prompt_templates.scenario: First-time audit of a single medical device technical file

prompt_templates.title: Detailed audit with regulatory references
prompt_templates.prompt: Perform a detailed MDR audit of the technical file at /path/to/device-folder for a Class III device. Use verbose mode and save the report to audit-report.json. Include the specific MDR article or annex reference for each finding.
prompt_templates.scenario: High-risk Class III device requiring thorough documentation review

prompt_templates.title: Batch multi-device audit
prompt_templates.prompt: Run a batch MDR audit using the config file at /path/to/batch-config.json. The config specifies three devices with different classifications. Report each device compliance status separately and highlight shared gaps across the portfolio.
prompt_templates.scenario: Auditing an entire product portfolio with multiple device classifications

prompt_templates.title: Remediation-focused gap analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit the Class IIb technical file at /path/to/device-folder. Focus on CER and PMS content completeness by examining file contents. For each finding, provide the specific MDR article reference and a remediation recommendation ranked by criticality. Identify which gaps would block a notified body submission.
prompt_templates.scenario: Pre-submission gap analysis with actionable remediation steps

output_examples.input: Audit Class IIa device technical file at /path/to/hip-implant
output: CRITICAL: Clinical Evaluation Report not found (MDR Annex XIV Part A)
MAJOR: PMS Plan lacks trend reporting mechanism (MDR Article 83)

output_examples.input: Batch audit of 3 devices using config.json with classifications IIb, I, and IIa
output: Device #1 (Insulin Pump, Class IIb): NON_COMPLIANT - 3 critical findings including missing SSCP and incomplete CER
Device #2 (Bandage, Class I): COMPLIANT - all 5 checks passed
Device #3 (Surgical Stapler, Class IIa): PARTIAL - 1 major finding, PMCF Plan is incomplete
Full detailed reports saved to output directory for each device

best_practices: Always confirm the device classification before running the audit to ensure the correct MDR checklist is applied for the risk class
Review generated findings against the original MDR regulatory text to validate that article and annex references are accurate for your device type
Use the batch config mode for portfolio-wide audits rather than running sequential individual checks to get consistent cross-device comparisons
anti_patterns: Treating the automated audit output as a substitute for a qualified regulatory affairs review or notified body conformity assessment
Running audits on devices after guessing the classification parameter instead of verifying the correct class from the intended purpose statement
Relying on the file-size-based completeness check alone without manually reviewing document content for accuracy and relevance

faq.question: What EU regulations does this skill check against?
faq.answer: It checks compliance with EU MDR 2017/745, including Annex XIV for clinical evaluation requirements, Article 83 and Annex III for post-market surveillance, and Annex XIV Part B for PMCF obligations.

faq.question: Can this skill read PDF and Microsoft Word documents?
faq.answer: Yes, the script can extract text from PDF and DOCX files. However, the extraction is simplified and may not capture content in tables, images, or complex formatting. Results should be verified manually.

faq.question: Does this skill require internet or network access?
faq.answer: No. All auditing runs on local files only. The skill does not make any external API calls, send data over the network, or require internet connectivity to function.

faq.question: What medical device classes are supported?
faq.answer: All four MDR risk classes are supported: Class I, Class IIa, Class IIb, and Class III. Each class has its own required document checklist based on the applicable regulatory requirements for that risk level.

faq.question: Can I audit multiple medical devices in one run?
faq.answer: Yes. Create a JSON configuration file listing each device path and its classification, then run the skill with the --config option. The tool will process all devices and report results individually.

faq.question: Does this skill provide regulatory or legal advice?
faq.answer: No. This skill automates documentation presence checks but does not interpret regulations, provide legal opinions, or replace a qualified regulatory affairs professional or notified body conformity assessment.
